text: For mild adolescent depression, guidelines support a period of active support and monitoring before starting medication. For moderate to severe depression, evidence-based psychotherapy (such as CBT or interpersonal therapy) and/or an antidepressant are recommended, with regular follow-up.
source: GLAD-PC: Guidelines for Adolescent Depression in Primary Care (Pediatrics, 2018)
tags: treatment therapy CBT IPT antidepressant moderate severe first-line adolescent teen options

topic: Safety: antidepressants and suicide risk in youth
text: Antidepressants carry an FDA boxed warning for an increased risk of suicidal thoughts and behavior in children, adolescents, and young adults, especially in the first weeks of treatment or after a dose change. Close monitoring is essential. Fluoxetine and escitalopram are the SSRIs FDA-approved for adolescent depression.
source: U.S. FDA boxed warning; FDA prescribing information
tags: medication antidepressant SSRI fluoxetine escitalopram suicide risk boxed warning safety monitoring side effects youth

text: Cognitive behavioral therapy (CBT) and interpersonal therapy (IPT) have the strongest evidence base for adolescent depression and are recommended as first-line or core components of treatment, alone or combined with medication.
source: NICE NG134; American Academy of Child & Adolescent Psychiatry (AACAP) practice parameters
tags: therapy psychotherapy CBT IPT counseling talk therapy first-line evidence adolescent

text: Best practice is to track depression severity over time with a validated, repeatable tool such as the PHQ-9 (or the adolescent PHQ-A), and to adjust the plan based on whether scores improve.
source: GLAD-PC; APA Clinical Practice Guideline for the Treatment of Depression
tags: phq-9 phq-a screening measurement track severity monitor follow-up scores tool

text: Universal screening for depression in adolescents aged 12 and older with a validated instrument is recommended when systems are in place to ensure diagnosis, treatment, and follow-up.
source: U.S. Preventive Services Task Force (USPSTF); AAP Bright Futures
tags: screening uspstf phq-a adolescent 12 detect early identify

text: For any young person with suicide risk, collaborative safety planning and reducing access to lethal means (securing or removing firearms and stockpiled medications) are standard, evidence-supported steps.
source: GLAD-PC; SAMHSA / Suicide Prevention Resource Center
tags: safety plan means restriction firearms medications lock crisis suicide risk protect

text: Involving family or caregivers in psychoeducation and the treatment plan is associated with better engagement and outcomes for depressed adolescents.
tags: family caregiver parent involvement psychoeducation engagement support home

text: Regular physical activity, sleep regulation, and behavioral activation (re-engaging in rewarding activities) are useful adjuncts that can support recovery alongside therapy or medication.
source: NICE NG134; APA Clinical Practice Guideline
tags: exercise sleep behavioral activation lifestyle adjunct routine activity wellbeing

topic: Biomarkers do not select treatment (important limitation)
text: No EEG, hormonal, or other biological marker is currently validated to choose a specific antidepressant or therapy for an individual patient. Biomarker findings are group-level associations, not individual clinical tests; treatment selection remains a clinical decision made with the patient.
source: Consensus of current evidence (see project RESEARCH.md); precision-psychiatry literature
tags: biomarker eeg cortisol hormone not validated treatment selection limitation honest clinical decision individual

text: Treatment choices should be made together with the adolescent and family, weighing preferences, prior response, side-effect profiles, and access — not by a single test or score.
tags: shared decision preference choice patient family weigh options access consent
